oneliner: The 'ticking' of a scandium-45 nucleus is 10 billion times more precise than the best atomic clocks.
description: Physicists excited scandium-45 nuclei with an X-ray laser and saw that in a solid crystal, vibrations hinder ideal precision. But the transition's natural purity promises nuclear clocks with an error of less than a second over the age of the universe. This opens the door to ultra-precise measurement
abstract: The nucleus of scandium-45 can produce ultra-precise "ticks" that surpass the best atomic clocks. Scientists tested how pure this ticking is inside a solid, using an X-ray laser. Surrounding atoms blur the signal at least 500 times. Imagine a perfect tuning fork muted by a hand — can we free the nuclear "sound"?
threads: Внутри ядра скандия-45 происходит почти идеально ровное «тиканье» — как маятник, качающийся без помех. Учёные заглянули в этот процесс и обнаружили, что в твёрдом материале вибрации немного смазывают ход часов. Это открытие может привести к созданию ядерных часов, которые не собьются даже за миллиарды лет.

title: Fullerene: A Ball That Shoots Single Photons
oneliner: Carbon-60 molecules embedded in ordinary plastic turn it into a reliable source of single photons at room temperature.
description: Cheap fullerenes added to plastic emit light strictly in single-photon packets — fast and fail-proof. This simple material opens the door to affordable quantum devices for secure communication and computing.
abstract: Imagine a light source that emits photons one at a time, like drops from a precisely adjusted faucet. Scientists placed carbon molecules (fullerenes) into plastic and obtained such a source at room temperature. This could make quantum technologies cheaper and more accessible. Could the future of secure communication be built from graphite and polystyrene?
threads: Представьте кран, из которого капает не вода, а свет строго по одному фотону. Фуллерен — углеродный шарик в простом пластике — делает это при комнатной температуре. Дёшево и сердито: квантовая связь выходит из лабораторий в быт.

title: Starquakes: The Birth of Cosmic Radio Signals
oneliner: For the first time, simulations show how the shaking of a magnetar's crust gives rise to powerful bursts of radio emission.
description: Magnetars are dead stars with colossal magnetic fields. Their crust can crack, sending a pulse into space that can outshine an entire galaxy. New calculations combine seismology and electrodynamics, tracing the full chain from the jolt to a fast radio burst.
abstract: Imagine the crust of a super-magnetic star cracking like ice on a lake. These tremors launch waves in the magnetic field, which then explode into bright radio signals. Could such waves explain the mysterious repeating bursts from the depths of the Universe?
threads: Мёртвая звезда размером с город вдруг вздрагивает — и её магнитное поле идёт рябью, как плёнка на барабане. Колебания превращаются в пучки частиц, мчащихся почти со скоростью света. Мы ловим их как загадочные радиовсплески из далёкого космоса. Учёные впервые проследили весь путь — от трещины в коре до вспышки.

title: White Dwarf Plays Hide-and-Seek with Cosmic Dust
oneliner: A dead star hides behind dust clouds every five hours — and these clouds can vanish without a trace for months.
description: The white dwarf ZTF J1944+4557 plays hide-and-seek: its light dims by 30% every 4 hours 58 minutes. The dust particles here are no smaller than 0.2 microns and they dim all colors of the spectrum equally. But the strangest twist: the pattern of eclipses changes faster than the orbit, and sometimes a
abstract: Astronomers have found a dead star (a white dwarf) that is periodically obscured by a swarm of debris — like a moth fluttering in front of a lamp. This is only the second such case ever. The most surprising part: the eclipses disappeared for half a year and then came back. What cosmic clockwork drives this mechanism?
threads: Белый карлик, мёртвая звезда размером с Землю, каждые 5 часов ныряет в облака собственной пыли, а потом вдруг снова становится чистым. Эта космическая игра в прятки помогает понять, как умирают планеты.

title: How a baby planet drinks gas: a new discovery
oneliner: For the first time, we've seen a forming planet greedily sucking in gas from a cosmic cloud.
description: A baby planet absorbing gas has been discovered around the star AB Aurigae. Light from hydrogen revealed its growth with a unique pattern—astronomers observed this for the first time in a planet embryo. This is direct evidence of how giants like Jupiter grow.
abstract: The star AB Aurigae has a baby planet, AB Aur b. Its glow resembles falling water: part of the light 'retreats,' part 'rushes in.' This pattern—like a whirlpool—suggests that the planet might be sucking in gas from its cosmic 'nursery.' This is only the second such case in the world and the first with this kind of light signature. The mystery is: what's actually making the planet shine like this?
threads: Астрономы застали экзопланету-младенца за «кормлением»: газ со скоростью 100 км/с падает на неё, создавая уникальный световой узор. Открытие у звезды AB Возничего — первое прямое указание на такой процесс у зародыша планеты. Это как увидеть, как строится газовый гигант вроде Юпитера.

title: Moon Bell: A New Way to Catch Gravitational Waves
oneliner: Moon Bell: How the stillness of our satellite will allow us to hear the gravitational hum of the Universe.
description: Scientists from the LILA mission aim to transform the Moon into a gravitational wave detector. Devoid of noise, the Moon will ring like a bell when hit by waves, and laser sensors will measure these vibrations. This opens the low-frequency band and lets us study black hole mergers right back to the
abstract: A lunar gravitational-wave detector: the quiet Moon resonates like a bell, amplifying signals from space. The LILA project uses lasers to hear black hole mergers and supernova explosions. The first phase is already sensitive enough, and the second will reach the edge of the observable universe. What whispers will the Moon carry to us?
threads: Луна – самый тихий уголок Солнечной системы. На ней нет землетрясений, океанов и ветра. Теперь физики поняли, как превратить эту тишину в гигантский детектор гравитационных волн, способный услышать слияния черных дыр с самого края Вселенной. 🚀🌖

title: Invisible Black Holes from Magnetic Fields
oneliner: Magnetic fields of the early Universe explain both dark matter and future gravitational waves.
description: Scientists have found a mechanism for the birth of primordial black holes from magnetic vortices in the early Universe. These invisible objects may make up dark matter, and their formation created gravitational waves detectable by future detectors.
abstract: Primordial black holes could explain mysterious dark matter. A fresh idea shows how they emerge from magnetic fields in the early Universe, leaving behind gravitational waves—ripples in spacetime that detectors will soon be able to catch. Could we finally be hearing the birth of invisible worlds?
threads: В ранней Вселенной бушевали магнитные бури. Они не только закрутили космический магнетизм, но и создали невидимые чёрные дыры. Эти дыры могут быть тёмной материей, а их рождение — породить гравитационные волны, которые скоро уловят детекторы.

title: An Invisible Intruder Smashed Into the Milky Way: The Impact Scar Found in Stars
oneliner: A stellar 'dent' has been discovered in the Milky Way — a telltale mark left by a dark galaxy invisible to telescopes.
description: In the Milky Way's stellar disk, astronomers spotted a region where hundreds of stars suddenly changed their speed. Nearby, gas clouds are crumpled into a giant 'finger,' and at its tip, there's not a single star. It's a dwarf galaxy made of dark matter that rammed our disk millions of years ago, le
abstract: Our Galaxy has a weird dent in the way stars are moving—like something invisible rammed right through the stellar disk. It lines up perfectly with a 'wound' in the gas clouds, like a bullet hole. Maybe a dark dwarf galaxy shot through the Milky Way. How many of these ghostly things are lurking around us?
threads: Звёзды в Млечном Пути «проваливаются» вниз из-за пролёта невидимой карликовой галактики. Эта тёмная «неудачница» не зажгла ни одной звезды, но оставила след в движении звёзд. Такие находки объясняют, как устроена тёмная материя.

title: Clouds of Molten Rock on an Alien Planet
oneliner: The James Webb Space Telescope has detected clouds of molten rock for the first time in the atmosphere of a planet heated to stellar temperatures.
description: On the dayside of WASP-121b, where rocks vaporize from the heat, Webb’s telescope has spotted clouds of calcium titanate — the same white pigment used in paint. The discovery unveils the alchemy of ultra-hot worlds and how the host star gradually destroys its planet.
abstract: Astronomers have studied the dayside of one of the hottest exoplanets, WASP-121b, using the James Webb Space Telescope. For the first time beyond our Solar System, they found clouds made of the mineral calcium titanate (CaTiO3), along with a slew of molecules. It's like reading a planet's chemical passport just by analyzing its glow. What other unexpected substances might be lurking in scorching worlds?
threads: На планете WASP-121b, где даже горы испаряются, телескоп Уэбба впервые разглядел облака из минеральной пыли. Адская жара превращает камень в газ, а газ — в светящуюся дымку. Открытие обещает разгадать тайны рождения и гибели сверхгорячих миров.

title: Black Hole Yields: Weak Spot Found
oneliner: Physicists have found for the first time an influence that actually deforms a black hole — a field of matter particles.
description: Black holes are not always perfectly rigid. Under the influence of a field of matter particles (such as electrons), they bend, although it was previously thought that weak influences couldn't deform them. Ordinary fields, like light or gravity, leave them unperturbed, but matter does not.
abstract: Black holes are famed for their invincibility: tidal forces can't deform them. But new research has turned up a surprising exception—fermion fields (matter particles) actually make a dent, like pressing a fingerprint into their event horizon. Why can matter 'touch' a black hole while radiation can't?
threads: Чёрные дыры оказались не такими уж каменными: впервые найдено воздействие, которое их деформирует — фермионное поле. Раньше считалось, что они непробиваемы для слабых полей, но материя всё-таки оставляет вмятину. Нарушение скрытых симметрий гравитации.

title: How Symmetry Saves Quantum Entanglement from Heat
oneliner: Usually high temperature breaks quantum bonds, but symmetry holds them even in scorching systems.
description: Physicists have discovered: if a quantum system obeys a strict symmetry rule—like charge conservation—its particles remain entangled even at infinitely high temperature. This finding explains how order survived the Big Bang and promises quantum devices that don’t fear overheating.
abstract: Quantum entanglement is like an invisible thread between particles. Usually, heat snaps it, and everything becomes just a mix of states. But scientists have proven that if particles follow a strict symmetry (like a tile pattern), entanglement survives even at high temperatures. Could symmetry serve as armor for the quantum world?
threads: Квантовая запутанность — как танец невидимых партнёров. Обычно жара разрывает их связь, но новое исследование показало: если партнёры обязаны держаться за руки (симметрия), они не расстаются никогда. Это путь к квантовым устройствам, устойчивым к нагреву.

title: Who’s Who in the World of Particles and Antiparticles
oneliner: A new algorithm identifies B mesons and their antipodes by the full decay picture, not by individual details, boosting accuracy by 35%.
description: Physicists at LHCb have learned to identify neutral B mesons—particles that can turn into their antiparticles—more precisely. The new method analyzes not individual traits but the entire decay picture, improving accuracy by 35%. This brings us closer to understanding why matter defeated antimatter i
abstract: Scientists have found a better way to distinguish between types of B mesons born in particle collisions. Just as you can tell what exploded from the pattern of debris, the new algorithm analyzes all the particles flying out. Accuracy improved by 35% for one type and by 20% for another. Could chaos hide this much order?
threads: При столкновении протонов рождаются близнецы: частица и её антимир. Раньше их различали, словно узнавая человека только по носу. Теперь, используя весь «рисунок» траекторий, физики улучшили распознавание на 35%. Маленький шаг к разгадке великой тайны антивещества.

title: Einstein's Paradox: Why the Quantum World Can't Be Fooled
oneliner: The old debate about bypassing quantum uncertainty is finally settled.
description: In 1935, Einstein devised a thought experiment that seemed to allow knowing both the exact position and speed of a particle at once. New work proves: any measurement shatters this illusion, and there's no way around the fundamental prohibition.
abstract: Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen thought you could outsmart quantum mechanics: learn the momentum without touching the particle, then measure its position. But new research shows it's impossible — any measurement always introduces uncertainty. It’s like trying to walk silently across a creaky floor: the quantum world always 'hears' your steps.
threads: Эйнштейн считал, что можно обмануть квантовую неопределённость. В 1935 году он придумал опыт, где две частицы раскрывают друг друга. Казалось, вот-вот падёт принцип Гейзенберга. Но свежий разбор доказал: измерение разрушает предсказание. Природа хитрее — никаких лазеек, квантовый мир стоит насмерть.

title: Red Dots Are Not Twins of Dusty Quasars
oneliner: Little red galaxies discovered by the James Webb telescope turned out to be nothing like bright dusty quasars.
description: The Webb telescope spotted a multitude of tiny red galaxies in the early Universe. They were confused with dazzlingly bright galaxies where a black hole heats dust like a blast furnace. Though their color 'portraits' are similar, the red dots have cooler dust—meaning their 'engines' run more modestl
abstract: JWST found many unusual «red dots»—dusty galaxies with V-shaped spectra. They seem similar to super-bright dusty quasars with a blue excess (BHDs). But a detailed comparison showed: the «dots» have far less dust and lack the hot glow from a black hole. So different roads lead to a similar outward appearance—like a mushroom and a coral.
threads: Крошечные красные точки, заполонившие снимки «Джеймса Уэбба», оказались не теми, кем казались. У них нет горячей пыли от прожорливых чёрных дыр — значит, в ранней Вселенной работал совсем другой механизм роста галактик. Это меняет картину первых миллиардов лет после Большого взрыва.

title: The Master Key to the Secrets of Spinning Black Holes
oneliner: Scientists found a way to replace mountains of equations with a single action — taking a derivative of a generating function.
description: Spinning black holes and neutron stars: their calculations used to be incredibly cumbersome. Scientists proposed a method where everything reduces to a generating function. It's like a universal dough — from it, by simple differentiation, you get any characteristic of the system. The new approach ha
abstract: Scientists invented a tool, kind of like a master key: it lets them easily and accurately figure out how any object interacts with a spinning black hole. With it, they’ve now fully mapped out the tidal effects on a neutron star across four levels of complexity. Why don’t black holes rip everything apart, and how do they let us peer in so deep?
threads: Физики нашли математический трюк: одна «производящая функция» заменяет горы вычислений у вращающейся чёрной дыры. Теперь сложнейший расчёт — это просто производная. Удалось детально увидеть, как чёрная дыра разрывает нейтронную звезду при встрече.

title: A Bond Beyond Time
oneliner: A muon experiment proved their connection ignores whether measurement or decay came first.
description: Muon pairs are born with strictly linked properties. It turns out the link holds even if one particle disintegrates before the other is measured. Our observations aren't a snapshot of reality, just a handy tool.
abstract: Scientists studied entangled muon pairs: even if one muon decays before the spin of the other is measured, their properties remain correlated. It's like two synchronized dancers who keep dancing together, no matter who started first. Does this mean our perception of time is just an illusion?
threads: Квантовая сцепка не знает, что такое «раньше» или «позже». Эксперимент с мюонами показал: взаимосвязь частиц сохраняется, даже если одна уже распалась. Для корреляций временной порядок — как цвет галстука у близнецов: не важно, кто первый его наденет, всё равно они одинаковы. Это наносит удар по привычной картине времени как потока событий.

title: Carbon Giant by a Dead Star
oneliner: JWST spotted a soot planet orbiting a pulsar: its carbon-rich atmosphere turns theories upside down.
description: A gas giant the size of Jupiter orbits the pulsar PSR J2322-2650b, heated to 1900°C. Its atmosphere is thick soot of carbon chains, not the usual water vapor. Carbon is a hundred times more abundant than oxygen compared to normal, and ten thousand times more than nitrogen. This doesn't fit the 'blac
abstract: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope studied a strange object the size of Jupiter but orbiting a dead star. It turned out that its atmosphere is filled with carbon molecules, like soot, rather than familiar gases. This discovery turns our ideas about what planets can be made of upside down. What other unexpected worlds does the cosmos hide?
threads: Вокруг мёртвой звезды-пульсара вращается планета, чья атмосфера состоит из сажи и углеродных молекул. Это удивительно: обычно такие миры — остатки звёзд — должны содержать много кремния и кислорода. Загадка углеродного гиганта бросает вызов астрофизике.

title: Cosmic Rain Will Improve Hurricane Forecasts
oneliner: Streams of invisible particles from space allow more accurate measurements of atmospheric density and early warnings of cyclones.
description: An invisible stream of particles from space helps assess atmospheric density. Scientists have shown that even a small muon detector can significantly improve hurricane predictions. This method provides a three-dimensional picture unattainable with ordinary pressure sensors.
abstract: Cosmic rays create invisible particles called muons, and their number at the ground tells us how dense the air is overhead. By measuring muons with a small detector, we can better see atmospheric density and improve weather forecasts for intense cyclones. It's like giving the sky a real-time scale—what else might these cosmic messengers reveal?
threads: Каждую секунду сквозь вас пролетают сотни невидимых стрел из космоса — мюонов. Их количество меняется от плотности воздуха. Учёные поняли, как с помощью этих частиц заранее узнавать о зарождающихся ураганах — и теперь даже маленький детектор улучшит прогнозы. Космос становится нашим метеорологом.

title: Neutrino Travelers from the Fifth Dimension
oneliner: Right-handed neutrinos from a micro-dimension leave sharp steps in electron energy — and the KATRIN experiment is ready to catch them.
description: Physicists have proposed a way to test the hypothesis of a fifth dimension the size of a bacterium. If right-handed neutrinos live in it, then by measuring the energy of electrons in KATRIN, we will see sharp steps — one or more. Their number and position will reveal the properties of the hidden wor
abstract: Imagine our Universe has a tiny hidden dimension, just a micron across. A special particle — the right-handed neutrino — could travel through it. The KATRIN experiment can spot its traces: if it's light, several 'steps' appear on the graph; if heavy, just one. So, can we 'see' extra dimensions right in the lab?
threads: В крошечных складках пространства могут прятаться нейтрино-невидимки. Каждое такое нейтрино рождает целую семью более тяжёлых двойников. Эксперимент KATRIN способен заметить их по резким изломам в энергии электронов. Если сигнал найдут, это станет окном в дополнительное измерение.

title: Spring Crystal: Giant Response Without Coils
oneliner: A bulk crystal produces inductance without wire coils or superconductors.
description: Crystal Mn3Si2Te6, exposed to a magnetic field and a weak current, pushes back like a spring refusing to let the current change. Inside, microscopic whirlpools of electron flow rearrange in sync. This could miniaturize components for quantum electronics without tricky fabrication.
abstract: Scientists have uncovered a new form of inductance (the capacity to store magnetic energy) in the crystal Mn3Si2Te6. Think of invisible current twisters spinning inside the material, pushing back hard against any change in the field. No intricate nanostructures needed—this phenomenon could lead to ultra-efficient devices. Picture a coil secretly embedded in a solid chunk of mineral. Pretty wild, right?
threads: Обнаружен кристалл, который превращается в гигантскую «электрическую пружину». Магнитное поле и слабый ток запускают в нём вихри электронных потоков, которые не хотят останавливаться и выдают мощный всплеск напряжения. Никаких катушек и сверхпроводников — такая встроенная индуктивность может сделать квантовые схемы намного компактнее.

title: How Acceleration Turns Vacuum into a Hot Bath
oneliner: The Unruh effect: an accelerating observer feels warmth from empty space.
description: A particle thermometer accelerated through vacuum heats up, and the way it reaches equilibrium depends on the field type and dimensions. Intriguingly, it heats faster than it cools, mimicking the Mpemba effect. This asymmetry provides a fingerprint to identify the Unruh effect.
abstract: Picture an astronaut in the void of space starting to accelerate. According to theory, the vacuum suddenly stops feeling cold and empty—instead, they sense warmth, as if they’ve plunged into a hot bath. Scientists studied exactly how this heating happens and found a weird asymmetry: 'heating' happens faster than 'cooling.' Why does the void heat you asymmetrically? It’s reminiscent of the Mpemba effect, where hot water freezes faster than cold.
threads: Пустой космос вовсе не пуст: если разогнаться до невообразимой скорости, вакуум нагреется. Физики выяснили, что такой нагрев идёт быстрее остывания — квантовый вариант эффекта Мпембы. Этот парадокс поможет построить детектор, который отличит тепло ускорения от обычного теплового потока.

title: Why is the Moon so similar to Earth?
oneliner: A new theory explains the Moon's chemical resemblance to Earth: in an ocean of molten rock, dense rocks sank and lighter ones rose.
description: According to the leading hypothesis, the Moon was born from a collision between Earth and a planet called Theia. However, calculations predicted the Moon should consist mostly of Theia’s material, yet it’s almost a chemical twin of Earth. A new model cracks the paradox: if Theia’s interior was dense
abstract: Imagine that after a cosmic collision between two worlds, their material separated like oil and water: the lighter proto-Earth material ended up on the outside, while the impactor’s material stayed inside. That’s how the Moon got its Earth-like appearance, even though it’s different on the inside. Could this explain why we don’t see traces of another planet?
threads: Луна — почти близнец Земли по изотопам, хотя возникла из обломков чужой планеты. Загадка решается просто: при застывании лунного океана магмы тяжёлые породы Тейи утонули, а лёгкие земные всплыли и затвердели сверху. Так что наш спутник — лишь притворщик с инопланетным нутром.

title: Seven Stars from One Dough
oneliner: Astronomers have for the first time seen seven baby stars born from a single disk, just like from a batch of dough.
description: In the nebula NGC 6334I, a system of seven protostars was discovered in a common gas disk. The disk, like fragile dough, tore apart under its own weight into pieces, and each condensed into a star. Previously, such a mechanism was thought to be common only for binary stars. The discovery proves that
abstract: Scientists found a system of seven stellar embryos, surrounded by a single disk of gas and dust. This disk, like a carousel spun too fast, breaks into separate clumps—that’s how 'stellar families' with many members are born. Previously, this was only seen in theory. What else do cosmic clouds hide?
threads: Газовый диск разрывается, как тесто для пиццы, и из каждого куска рождается звезда. Впервые засняли процесс с семью «близнецами» одновременно. Это переписывает учебники по рождению звёзд: раньше думали, что так появляются лишь пары, а оказалось — можно сразу семерых.

title: The Higgs Field Opens a Passage Through a Black Hole
oneliner: In extreme gravity, the Higgs field gives birth to antigravity, turning a black hole into a traversable region.
description: Usually passive, the Higgs field inside a black hole gives rise to antigravity, preventing collapse into a point and creating a tunnel. The i(SM+GR) theory unites particle physics and gravity, predicting traversable black holes and the possibility of a universe before the Big Bang.
abstract: Usually, the Higgs field across the Universe is like a calm ocean surface, giving particles their mass. But near black holes, where gravity is monstrous, this field starts to churn, creating an antigravity effect. This stops spacetime from collapsing into a point and instead opens a passage through the singularity. What if black holes aren't dead ends but portals to other parts of the cosmos?
threads: Внутри чёрной дыры поле Хиггса сходит с ума: вместо того, чтобы просто давать частицам массу, оно генерирует антигравитацию и размыкает сингулярность, превращая её в портал. Возможно, так можно путешествовать сквозь чёрные дыры и увидеть, что было до Большого взрыва.

title: How Acceleration Heats Up the Void
oneliner: Rapid acceleration makes empty space radiate heat—physicists have found a way to spot it.
description: Physicists have simulated the radiation that appears when electrons are suddenly accelerated by a laser. This 'heat from nothing,' predicted by the Unruh effect, was teased out from other signals using ultra-precise simulations and extremely small observation angles. The finding brings us closer to
abstract: According to the Unruh effect, an accelerating observer sees the vacuum as a warm bath. Although direct observation hasn't been achieved yet, new simulations of electron collisions with powerful lasers reveal where we might spot this warmth. For future facilities like LUXE, the chances increase a hundredfold. So could emptiness actually be warm?
threads: Пустое пространство не такое холодное, как кажется: если разогнаться почти до скорости света, вакуум засияет тепловым излучением. Физики рассчитали, как поймать этот призрачный свет в лазерных экспериментах: возможно, уже в ближайшие годы мы увидим «тепло от ничто».

title: Time from Disorder: How Atoms Revealed an Internal Clock
oneliner: Physicists have shown: time is measured not by ticking, but by the growth of disorder inside a system.
description: Scientists split a cloud of atoms and, by tracking rising disorder, ordered events in one half without seeing the other. This confirms that time emerges from internal changes. Such an approach will help us understand how time flows in black holes and right after the Big Bang.
abstract: Physicists ran an experiment with a cloud of ultracold atoms split by a barrier to test: can we ditch the usual clocks and measure time by internal changes within a system? They showed that the growth of disorder (entropy) in one part of the cloud perfectly orders events in the other—just as a pendulum in a closed room can replace a wall clock. Maybe time is just a convenient illusion born from the changes happening inside us?
threads: Что, если время — не тиканье незримых часов, а ощущение, рождающееся из перемен? Физики взяли облачко атомов, разделили лазером надвое и заставили одну половину жить по внутреннему распорядку. Оказалось, без внешнего секундомера события чётко упорядочиваются — достаточно следить за ростом беспорядка. Такой «энтропийный» подход объясняет, как течёт время в чёрных дырах и сразу после Большого взрыва, где привычным часам не на что опереться.

title: Cosmic Necklace Reveals Secrets of Galaxy Birth
oneliner: Three colliding galaxies ejected gas that curled into glowing beads on an invisible thread — this is how a cosmic giant is born.
description: Astronomers peered into the early Universe and saw how three massive galaxies, merging, eject gas. This gas compresses into clumps resembling necklace beads and glows brightly. These cosmic 'jewelry' pieces are an important stage in the construction of the most massive galaxies.
abstract: Astronomers peered into the distant past and saw how a giant galaxy is born. At its center, gas streams ejected during collisions tear into clumps, like beads on a string. This discovery shows that the largest galaxies in the universe could have assembled quickly and violently. What else will these cosmic 'beads' tell us?
threads: В далёком космосе три огромные галактики врезаются друг в друга, как мегаполисы, сливающиеся в один. От ударов разлетаются светящиеся газовые «бусины», нанизанные на невидимую нить. Это буйство рождения звёзд и, возможно, способ, которым возникли самые массивные галактики во Вселенной.

title: Black hole merger confirms Hawking's area law
oneliner: The total horizon area of two merging black holes became larger — exactly as Hawking's law predicts.
description: A clear signal from the merger of two black holes, each about 33 solar masses, confirmed Hawking's theorem: the final horizon area turned out larger than the sum of the initial ones. This strengthens the link between black hole area and entropy.
abstract: The LIGO gravitational-wave observatory caught a signal from the merger of two black holes of almost equal mass — about 33 solar masses each. The collision gave birth to a single hole, and its event horizon area turned out to be larger than the sum of the original areas, exactly following Hawking's law. It's as if two whirlpools merged into one, and their boundaries didn't shrink. Could this rule be violated under extreme conditions?
threads: Две чёрные дыры весом по 33 Солнца слились в одну. Их общая «поверхность» — горизонт событий — стала больше, как и требует закон Хокинга. Площадь чёрных дыр только растёт, подобно энтропии. Возможно, это ключ к стреле времени.

title: Topological Physics at Room Temperature Using Light
oneliner: Physicists have realized the Haldane model without ultra-low temperatures for the first time, thanks to a laser lattice.
description: Exotic quantum states typically exist only in the chill of liquid helium. But here, scientists replaced cooling with a light lattice, where atoms move in unison like an orchestra. The collective glow drowns out random noise, making it impossible for chaos to break through. They not only reproduced t
abstract: Scientists have found a way to create a special quantum state (topological phase) at room temperature, even though it previously required deep cooling. They used the collective light emission of atoms, like the synchronized flashing of fireflies, to protect the system from thermal noise. This 'warm' approach paves the way for robust quantum technologies—imagine a computer that isn't afraid of overheating?
threads: Обычно экзотические квантовые состояния требуют холода ниже, чем в космосе. Но физики обманули тепловой шум, заставив атомы петь в унисон в луче лазера. Теперь неуловимые топологические фазы живут при комнатной температуре — и обещают квантовые устройства без криостатов.

title: Quark Captivity: The Secret of the Invisible String's Snap
oneliner: A computer simulation reveals how the bonds holding quarks break, and the unexpected twists that come with it.
description: Using a simulation, scientists studied how the force strings connecting quarks break. The rupture turned out to be more complex: entire families of particles are born, and in a dense environment the process slows down. This discovery will help understand the microworld and can be tested on special d
abstract: Imagine quarks inside a proton tied by a stretchy rubber band. Pull hard enough and it snaps, birthing new particles. Using a supercomputer, scientists explored how such strings break in a theory closer to reality. They found a "traffic jam" effect: when particles pile up, breaking stalls and strings can knot up. Why would nature bother with such knotty tricks?
threads: Кварки, замкнутые в протоне, соединены невидимой струной, как бусинки на резинке. Когда струна лопается, рождаются сразу несколько новых частиц. Это открытие приближает нас к управлению микромиром.

title: Music from Quantum Randomness
oneliner: Scientists turned quantum randomness into live music.
description: By measuring the random properties of entangled light particles, scientists and artists create an audiovisual show that nature itself prevents from ever repeating.
abstract: Using entangled particles, scientists created music that sounds as unpredictable as a jazz improvisation by the atoms themselves. This shows how quantum randomness becomes art. Could such music help us feel the weirdness of the quantum world?
threads: В одном зале свет рождает запутанные фотоны. Их танец, случайный, но связанный, управляет звуком и видео на сцене. Музыка, которую нельзя повторить — её дирижёром становится сама квантовая природа.

title: Axions Connect Two Cosmic Radio Mysteries
oneliner: Axions turning into radio waves explain the excess cosmic radio noise and the mysterious dip in the hydrogen signal.
description: Two cosmic mysteries—the excess of radio waves in the sky (ARCADE2) and the anomalously weak signal from the earliest hydrogen (EDGES)—have received a common explanation. The culprit could be axions, ultralight dark matter particles. In primordial magnetic fields, axions turn into radio waves, creat
abstract: Two anomalies in radio observations — an excess of radio waves (ARCADE2) and strong absorption at the 21 cm line (EDGES) — could be explained by axions converting into photons in the early Universe. Lightweight axions in magnetic fields, like a tiny radio station, produce extra radio waves and at the same time weaken the signal from ancient hydrogen. Could the cosmos be talking to us on the frequencies of invisible particles?
threads: Вселенная наполнена невидимым радиошумом, а древний сигнал водорода имеет странный провал. Возможно, виновник — аксионы: частицы-призраки, которые иногда превращаются в радиоволны, как если бы маятник раскачался от невидимых толчков. Это открытие может объяснить сразу две загадки и дать ключ к темной материи.

title: Binary Stars Build Dark Matter Molecules
oneliner: Two swirling stars gather dark matter into a giant cloud that resembles a molecule.
description: Simulations show that a cloud of dark matter around two stars takes the shape of a molecule. The elongated orbits of the stars knock particles out of the cloud, causing their trajectories to round out. This process could leave a noticeable imprint in the gravitational-wave background, helping to rev
abstract: Around a pair of tightly orbiting stars, ultralight particles can gather into something like a giant 'molecule.' As the stars close in, this structure starts losing particles (ionizing), which affects their orbit more than gravitational waves do. It's like a cloud speeding up and shaking off droplets — except here it alters the dance of the stars themselves.
threads: Две звезды танцуют, и между ними рождается невидимая гигантская молекула из тёмной материи. Когда их орбиты колеблются, это космическое творение сбрасывает частицы, как встряхнутый атом теряет электроны. Такой процесс может оставить уникальный отпечаток в ряби пространства-времени — ключ к разгадке тёмной материи.

title: Cosmic Clocks Diverge: A New Mystery of the Universe's Expansion
oneliner: Measuring the expansion rate using Cepheids yielded 71.1 (km/s)/Mpc — the conflict with other methods has only intensified.
description: A new study used only pulsating Cepheid stars to calculate the expansion rate of the Universe. The resulting value of 71.1 (km/s)/Mpc turned out to be lower than the previous 'gold standard' and even further from the Planck satellite data. This sharpens the 'Hubble tension' and suggests our knowledg
abstract: The Universe is expanding, but scientists can't agree on how fast. Imagine you're measuring how quickly a balloon inflates by looking at dots on its surface — and your rulers give different results. A new study used only data on Cepheids (pulsating stars) and obtained a lower expansion rate than before, hinting at hidden errors in measurements. Maybe we're misreading the galaxies' own motions?
threads: Скорость расширения Вселенной снова перемерили. Новое число — 71.1 км/с/Мпк — почти на 2 ниже прежнего «золотого стандарта». Расхождение с данными спутника «Планк» стало ещё значительнее. Это намекает на неполноту наших знаний о космосе.

title: Black Hole or Impostor: The Ring of the Final Plunge
oneliner: The gravitational 'ringing' of infalling matter gives away whether a black hole is genuine.
description: By analyzing the final 'plunge' of matter, scientists search for spectral signatures of impostors. The fakes produce a ringing: a comb of resonances at low frequencies and a sharp cutoff at high ones. Stacking signals from many events lets them hear even the quietest notes.
abstract: Scientists have proposed a way to tell a real black hole from an imitator. When an object falls into such an imitator, special signals arise — like different bell rings. Individually, the signals are weak, but by collecting data from many falls, you can confidently identify a fake. What lies behind the event horizon?
threads: Падая в черную дыру, объект издает гравитационный «звон». Если дыра — подделка, звон меняется, как у колокольчиков из разного металла. Ученые нашли способ услышать разницу. Это поможет проверить, настоящие ли черные дыры во Вселенной.

title: Black Hole Wind Clears the Galactic Center
oneliner: For the first time, scientists have seen the supermassive black hole at the Milky Way’s heart unleashing a hot wind.
description: Astronomers found the 'breath' of the black hole at the center of the Milky Way. A huge cone of blown-out cold hydrogen, over three light-years long, proved that black holes not only devour matter but also clear space with powerful winds.
abstract: At the center of our Galaxy lies a supermassive black hole. Scientists have discovered that it 'blows out' a huge cone in the surrounding gas cloud, like a hairdryer pushing aside fluff. This discovery helps us understand how black holes affect their galaxies. What else might the heart of the Milky Way be hiding?
threads: В центре нашей Галактики чёрная дыра «включила фен». Она извергает горячий ветер, который разметает холодные облака газа. Это объясняет, почему вокруг неё так пусто, и помогает понять, как чёрные дыры управляют целыми галактиками.

title: Quantum Compass in the Labyrinth of Solutions
oneliner: A hybrid quantum algorithm finds better solutions for complex problems faster than classical counterparts.
description: Scientists applied a hybrid quantum algorithm to the Ising model—a problem of finding the most stable state of many interacting parts. A processor of 104 superconducting qubits found configurations with lower energy than the classical annealing method. Calculations show that with 100 qubits, a quant
abstract: Like finding the best seating plan at a wedding, some optimization problems are extremely hard. Quantum computers can smartly sample the best arrangements instead of checking each one. With just 104 qubits, a new quantum method beat a top classical algorithm. Could near-term quantum machines soon crack real-world logistics and scheduling puzzles?
threads: Обычный компьютер ищет минимум энергии, как шарик, катящийся в ямку, и может застрять в луже. Квантовый процессор из 100 кубитов действует иначе: он прощупывает все долины разом, словно вода, мгновенно находящая самый глубокий сток. Это приближает эру квантового превосходства в решении повседневных задач — от расписаний до логистики.

title: Surfer Photons: How a Wave Guides a Particle
oneliner: An experiment with single photons revealed hidden trajectories predicted by pilot-wave theory.
description: In pilot-wave theory, particles follow clear trajectories, like surfers on waves. By reconstructing photon trajectories in the double-slit experiment, physicists saw something strange: in the dark fringes, the particle's mass behaved like a tachyon's — becoming imaginary. This is the first direct ev
abstract: Scientists tracked the paths of single photons in the double-slit experiment and saw that in the dark spots, their “effective mass” becomes negative — as if the particle momentarily takes on the properties of a tachyon, capable of outrunning light. This is the first direct evidence of relativistic effects in the nearly century-old pilot-wave theory. Could a strict order be hiding behind the apparent randomness of the quantum world?
threads: Представьте лодку на волне-пилоте. Такую картину мира предложили де Бройль и Бом. Физики впервые проследили путь фотона в двухщелевом опыте и нашли частицы с отрицательной массой — тахионы, нарушающие привычные законы. Это меняет понимание реальности.

title: Quantum Whisper in Diamond
oneliner: Using diamond defects, physicists have overheard the collective whisper of spins, suppressing light noise by 3.8 decibels.
description: Using NV centers—glowing defects in diamond—scientists directly captured the mutual influence of spins. Reducing measurement noise paves the way for ultrasensitive quantum sensors.
abstract: Scientists have learned to read the magnetic states of countless defects in diamond with record accuracy, suppressing light noise and nearly reaching the ultimate limit—the intrinsic quantum noise of the particles. It’s like picking out a whisper in a crowded room. How will this reshape quantum technologies?

title: Moving Atoms Slash Errors in Quantum Computers
oneliner: Scientists showed that allowing neutral atoms to move can cut errors in quantum computing by several times.
description: Errors in quantum computers are inevitable. A new approach with moving neutral atoms lets logical qubits correct errors on the spot, boosting accuracy up to 8-fold.
abstract: Scientists built a quantum computer from atoms that can be moved and linked on the spot—like moving pieces on a board and having them instantly understand each other. This resulted in 2–8 times fewer errors in three tests: factoring a number, chaining operations, and correcting errors. Might this approach lead to quantum machines as flexible as a regular computer?
threads: Движущиеся атомы могут сделать квантовый компьютер до 8 раз точнее. Вместо неподвижного ожидания кубиты перемещаются и взаимодействуют на ходу, как танцоры, всегда оказываясь в нужном месте. Такой трюк снижает ошибки и приближает квантовые вычисления к реальным задачам.

title: The Hydrogen Secret of Young Sub-Neptunes
oneliner: In the depths of young sub-Neptunes, hydrogen and molten rock mix like cocoa in milk, forever changing the planet’s appearance.
description: Scientists have built the first model of sub-Neptune interiors where hydrogen and rock are not separated but mixed. It turns out that in young planets, hydrogen makes up tens of percent of the mass and, as it escapes outward, slows down contraction. This explains why juvenile sub-Neptunes are larger
abstract: Scientists found that inside sub-Neptunes, hydrogen and molten rock can mix like syrup and water. That means young planets keep hydrogen locked deep down, which slows their shrinking — they 'breathe' more slowly than we used to think. So, what does this blending mean for finding life?
threads: Оказывается, внутри молодых субнептунов водород и расплавленный камень смешиваются, словно горячий шоколад с молоком. Когда планета остывает, водород «выпотевает» наружу, замедляя её сжатие. Это открытие меняет картину того, как растут и эволюционируют планеты-гиганты.

title: Hot Cools Down Faster: The Quantum Paradox
oneliner: Scientists have witnessed for the first time how intense excitation in the microworld settles faster than a nearly calm state.
description: Like a bonfire dying quicker than smoldering embers, the more excited a quantum system, the faster it relaxes. Physicists confirmed this with nuclear spins—tiny atomic magnets. A paradox first noticed by a schoolboy in his kitchen now explains quantum behavior and promises to turbocharge error corre
abstract: Imagine that hot water sometimes freezes faster than cold water. A similar paradox, called the Mpemba effect, has been found in the quantum world: particles that are highly excited can calm down faster than those that are almost at rest. Scientists have seen this for the first time under natural conditions without any external interference. So, maybe nature loves taking non-obvious paths?
threads: Горячее остывает быстрее холодного — этот парадокс добрался до квантового мира. Физики увидели, как возбуждённые ядерные спины успокаиваются скорее, чем почти спокойные. Без вмешательства, само собой. Возможно, будущие квантовые компьютеры будут использовать этот эффект для мгновенного сброса ошибок.

title: Dark Forces Twisted the Light from the Past
oneliner: The oldest light in the universe reached us slightly twisted — possibly rotated by a dark matter field.
description: Astronomers found that the cosmic microwave background — the afterglow of the Big Bang — has slightly changed its polarization angle over billions of years. This twist may be caused by interaction with dark matter or dark energy. Data from the ACT telescope backs earlier hints from WMAP and Planck.
abstract: Scientists studied the light left over from the Big Bang and noticed that its polarization is slightly twisted — as if the light passed through a giant cosmic filter. This hints at the influence of dark matter or dark energy. So what else is hidden in this ancient light?
threads: Самый древний свет во Вселенной пришёл к нам слегка повёрнутым — как будто его закрутило невидимое поле. Астрономы заподозрили тёмную материю. Если подтвердится, мы впервые увидим следы того, из чего состоит большая часть космоса.

title: Radio Whisper of Dark Matter: A New Axion Detector
oneliner: A layered 'sandwich' of special materials turns invisible particles into light and promises to catch dark matter.
description: Scientists have created a detector that catches axions—candidates for dark matter. The device, made of layered material in a magnetic field, turns axions into light particles. Resonance, like that of a musical instrument, amplifies the faint signal, and tilting instead of moving parts lets you tune
abstract: Dark matter might consist of ultra-light particles called axions. Scientists have developed a detector made of semiconductor layers, resembling a tunable antenna: as an axion passes through it, it transforms into faint light that can be detected. This will allow us to 'see' the invisible scaffolding of the Universe and come closer to solving the mystery of dark matter. Will such an antenna be able to pick up a signal from the cosmic web?

title: Quantum Logic of the Brain: No Particles, No Fields
oneliner: Nerve cells create a quantum-like entanglement of thoughts without any microscopic particles — just the synchronization of electrical waves.
description: The brain oscillates between options like a quantum particle, but without any physical quanta. A mathematical model has shown that quantum-like states emerge from coordinated neural activity. This can be tested with EEG, turning philosophical debates about consciousness into observable science.
abstract: Scientists are exploring how brain activity generates our thoughts and decisions. To do this, they borrow ideas from quantum physics, showing that neural networks can create 'entangled' states, akin to the link between particles. Imagine: two people thinking the exact same thing at the same time, without any communication—something similar may happen in the brain. What if our thoughts can actually become 'entangled'?
threads: Ваш мозг при выборе между кофе и чаем колеблется, как квантовая частица. Нейроны создают запутанные состояния без физических квантов — достаточно синхронизации ритмов. Скоро ЭЭГ покажет, как мысль «ходит по кругу», словно в квантовом лабиринте.

title: Electron on Helium: First Step Toward Reading a Quantum Bit
oneliner: For the first time, scientists achieved resonance between the motion of an electron on superfluid helium and a microwave field.
description: A device was built where a single electron on a helium film and a superconducting resonator exchange energy faster than quantum properties decay. This allows reading the electron’s state without destroying it—a crucial step toward quantum computers.
abstract: Picture electrons floating on the surface of liquid helium, like tiny boats. For the first time, scientists have 'hitched' one such electron to a microwave signal so strongly that they began to oscillate as one. This is a crucial step toward building quantum computers with these exotic particles. What else can we make dance to the rhythm of quantum waves?
threads: Электроны, плавающие на сверхтекучем гелии, — идеальные кубиты: холод, изоляция, никакого шума. Но измерить один электрон было невозможно. Теперь учёные «связали» его движение с микроволновым фотоном — как передать ритм через натянутую струну. Шаг к масштабируемому квантовому компьютеру на гелии.

title: Dense Fog Instead of a Cosmic Hurricane
oneliner: A neutron star was found to have a dense, fog-like wind — a discovery that challenges current theories.
description: The XRISM telescope discovered that the wind from neutron star GX 13+1 is not fast but exceptionally dense and slow. This changes our understanding of how such outflows are born and their impact on galaxies.
abstract: In a binary system, a neutron star pulls in material, forming a glowing disk. The disk gives rise to a wind — a stream of gas flowing away. Scientists have for the first time observed an unexpectedly dense and slow wind from the source GX 13+1, even though the star shines very brightly. It's as if a powerful lamp created a gentle breeze, not a hurricane. Why is it so quiet?
threads: Нейтронная звезда GX 13+1 удивила астрономов: вместо ожидаемого сверхбыстрого ветра она порождает медленный и очень плотный поток — настоящий космический туман. Это открытие меняет понимание того, как такие объекты влияют на целые галактики.

title: The Crooked Seed That Spawned the Universe
oneliner: The tiny difference between left and right gets amplified billions of times during crystallization, and perhaps explains why matter beat antimatter.
description: New theoretical research reveals how the weak interaction—normally imperceptible—gets massively amplified during crystal growth. The amplification depends on a critical number of atoms in the seed of the new phase. This mechanism might have played a decisive role in the early universe, turning a mic
abstract: Weak interactions create an energy difference between left-handed and right-handed molecules. Normally it's negligible, but during phase transitions (like water freezing) it gets amplified billions of times. A new model links this amplification to the size of the phase nucleus. Could the same mechanism explain why the Universe is mostly made of matter?
threads: Крошечный перекос между левым и правым, создаваемый слабым взаимодействием, может усилиться в миллиарды раз при кристаллизации — и, возможно, объяснить, почему мы состоим из материи, а не из антиматерии.

title: A Cosmic Blast Showed How Its Light Is Born
oneliner: Scientists figured out why the light from the most powerful cosmic explosion was rapidly losing energy: it was playing billiards with electrons.
description: For the first time in a gamma-ray burst GRB 221009A, astronomers noticed how the light fingerprints of elements were born and reddened. It turned out that particles of light emerged when matter met antimatter, and then, colliding with electrons, lost energy — like a billiard ball after a series of h
abstract: The brightest cosmic explosion, GRB 221009A, revealed a bizarre set of spectral lines that changed energy over time. Scientists explain it as light passing through a 'fog' of particles that cools it down, like a flashlight beam dimming in mist. Why did the lines shift so dramatically—from 37 to 6 million electronvolts—and what does that tell us about the blast's power?
threads: Один из ярчайших взрывов во Вселенной породил загадочные световые узоры. Оказалось, их создаёт «бильярд» из частиц: фотоны рождаются при аннигиляции и теряют энергию в столкновениях, словно шары. Это объяснило, почему свечение менялось. Возможно, в будущем мы сможем заглянуть в самое сердце таких катастроф.

title: Einstein’s Cosmic Cross Unveils the Secrets of Dark Matter
oneliner: A perfect mirage of four clone galaxies points to invisible mass.
description: Light from a distant galaxy, bent by a foreground cluster, creates four identical images in the sky. Ordinary matter alone wasn’t enough for such a trick—only by adding invisible dark matter did scientists achieve a match with observations. Now this cross serves as a natural telescope into the unive
abstract: A distant galaxy, HerS-3, appeared as an “Einstein cross” of five images—its light warped by the gravity of a galaxy cluster. Observations confirmed it's a single object and helped reveal an invisible clump of dark matter. Nature has gifted us a giant lens to peer into an era of furious star formation. What other cosmic mirages will tell us more?
threads: На небе нашли галактику, которая появляется сразу в четырёх местах. Это не иллюзия, а гравитационное искривление света невидимой тёмной материей. Такой космический крест помогает изучать раннюю Вселенную и доказывает: без тёмной материи не обойтись.

title: The Birth of a Gravastar: How a Star Escapes a Black Hole
oneliner: A star's collapse can end not as a black hole, but as a gravastar—a bubble of dark energy.
description: New simulations show that a dying star can turn into a glowing bubble—a gravastar. At its core, a microscopic seed of dark energy emerges, expanding and halting the collapse exactly at the boundary where a black hole would normally form. Researchers pinpointed the key condition: a sufficiently 'fluf
abstract: What if a black hole isn't the only ending for a dying star? Scientists have shown how, during the collapse of a dust cloud, a tiny region of repulsive energy can emerge at the center. It grows, slows down near the event horizon, and freezes, forming a gravastar — a stable object without a hole. Could such ghost stars already exist out there?
threads: Умирающая звезда сжимается, но в последний момент внутри неё рождается пузырёк тёмной энергии. Он растёт и останавливает коллапс, превращая звезду в гравастар — вечный светящийся шар без дна чёрной дыры. Новое исследование впервые показывает, как это возможно, и даёт рецепт создания таких объектов.

title: Earth as a Dark Matter Detector
oneliner: Earth's magnetic field helps search for elusive dark photons.
description: Analysis of years-long records of Earth's magnetic field has set the tightest limits on dark photons — dark matter particle candidates. Using the ionosphere as a giant resonator, scientists amplified faint magnetic fluctuations hundreds of times, but no direct signal has been found yet.
abstract: Earth helped search for dark matter. Its surface and ionosphere form a natural resonator that amplifies magnetic signals from dark photons. Analysis of years of geomagnetic data revealed no traces, but did set new strict limits. Imagine: our planet is a giant antenna listening to the cosmos. What other secrets do its magnetic records hold?
threads: Магнитное поле Земли дрожит на сверхнизких частотах — возможно, так с нами говорит тёмная материя. Учёные превратили планету в детектор и нашли способ усилить сигнал в сотни раз, используя ионосферу как зеркало резонатора. Новые данные сжали область поиска тёмных фотонов.

title: Silent Explosion: The Faintest Supernova in the Galaxy
oneliner: Nebula Pa 30 emits a thousand times fewer radio waves than expected — the quietest supernova remnant.
description: In 1181, Chinese astronomers spotted a flash — likely the merger of two white dwarfs. Modern observations of the Pa 30 nebula barely pick up a radio signal. This means the explosion’s energy was extremely low, or particle acceleration works differently. The discovery forces us to rethink the nature
abstract: The Pa 30 nebula is famous for its ultra-fast wind. First radio observations showed it's nearly invisible in radio waves, like a whisper in a noisy room. It's either the quietest supernova remnant or not a remnant at all. The mystery of the 1181 explosion remains.
threads: Представьте космический взрыв, который почти не слышно. Туманность Pa 30, возможно, родилась в 1181 году, но современные радиотелескопы улавливают лишь тишину. Если это сверхновая, то самая слабая из известных. Открытие намекает, что Галактика полна незаметных «призраков».

title: Invisible Lighthouse: Magnetar Pulse in a Gamma-Ray Burst
oneliner: In gamma-ray burst 230307A, for a split second, the beam of a celestial lighthouse flashed—likely the trail of a rapidly spinning neutron star.
description: The collision of a pair of ultra-dense stars produced the explosion GRB 230307A, which lasted unusually long. In its glow, for a split second, a rhythmic signal at 909 Hz emerged—like the beam of a celestial lighthouse. This pointed not to a black hole but to the birth of a rapidly spinning neutron
abstract: When neutron stars merge, they usually give birth to a black hole, but sometimes a super-dense magnetic top — a magnetar — can form. Scientists found rhythmic pulses in a gamma-ray burst at 909 times per second, similar to such a top's spin. Could this be a real 'cosmic lighthouse,' flickering in X-rays?
threads: Всего на долю секунды в гамма-всплеске мелькнул сигнал, похожий на биение сердца. Частота 909 Гц — это вращение новорождённой нейтронной звезды-магнетара. Такие космические «маяки» могут объяснить самые мощные взрывы во Вселенной.

title: Hydrogen and Antihydrogen Weave into a New Particle
oneliner: Scientists have shown that a strange state in the Standard Model is made of familiar atoms.
description: In particle theory, there’s a puzzling phenomenon that usually calls for a new particle. But the authors found that this role falls to ordinary hydrogen and antihydrogen, forming a special combination. The discovery brings the atomic world and fundamental forces closer together.
abstract: The Standard Model predicts the existence of an unusual state — η_W. It turned out that this is not a new particle, but a special combination of a hydrogen atom and an antihydrogen atom in their ground states. Nature's puzzle was solved using the most ordinary matter. What else is hidden in familiar atoms?
threads: Водород и антиводород, словно танцоры, сплетаются в новое состояние — и это не фантазия, а строгая теория. Учёные показали, что загадочная частица в Стандартной модели на самом деле состоит из знакомых атомов. Переворот в понимании антиматерии.

title: Sulfur in the Clouds: Webb Spies a Super-Neptune's Atmosphere
oneliner: The James Webb Space Telescope has sniffed out sulfur dioxide—the gas that smells like burnt matches—in a warm Neptune's atmosphere for the first time, proving that stellar ultraviolet light 'cooks' chemistry on planets across a range of temperatures.
description: Webb studied the planet HAT-P-26 b and found water, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide in its air. Previously, SO₂ had only been spotted on scorching hot giants or chilly sub-Neptunes; now it's clear that photochemistry works across a wide temperature range. A trend emerged: the higher the atmospher
abstract: The James Webb Space Telescope studied the atmosphere of planet HAT-P-26 b, which is slightly larger than Neptune. It found water, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide (which smells like a burnt match). This shows that even on cooler planets, active chemistry is at play. Imagine a planet where the air reeks of just-struck matches—what else could be going on there?
threads: Телескоп «Джеймс Уэбб» учуял запах горелых спичек в небе планеты HAT-P-26 b. Это диоксид серы — верный признак того, что ультрафиолет звезды постоянно перекраивает её атмосферу. Такая «световая кухня» работает даже на планетах холоднее 1000°С, а значит, химическое разнообразие миров гораздо шире, чем казалось.

title: The Cosmic Lighthouse Has Switched
oneliner: A supermassive black hole in a distant galaxy has started emitting X-ray flares half as often and four times more powerful than before.
description: The Ansky black hole in a distant galaxy has changed the rhythm of its X-ray flares: they are now half as frequent, but four times as powerful and longer lasting. Scientists link this to an object that might be making its final loops before falling into the hole, disturbing the disk of superheated g
abstract: In a distant galaxy, mysterious X-ray flares have been detected, repeating every few days. New observations show that over the course of a year, these flares became four times more energetic and twice as long. It's as if a cosmic lighthouse suddenly cranked up its signal — what changed in the black hole's vicinity?
threads: Космический маяк переключился: черная дыра в далекой галактике теперь выдает рентгеновские вспышки раз в 10 дней, вдвое реже, но вчетверо мощнее. Возможно, так ведет себя звезда на последнем витке перед поглощением.

title: Solar Eruption's Magnetic Field 'Heard' for the First Time
oneliner: Astronomers 'heard' a solar eruption's magnetic field for the first time by catching its radio signal.
description: By the frequency of radio waves from a cloud of solar gas, scientists directly measured its magnetic field. It turns out that the invisible force is hidden in tangled clumps, not evenly smeared across the cloud.
abstract: Scientists have captured a radio echo from magnetic islands inside a solar eruption for the first time. It's like spotting tiny magnets in a flying plasma cloud. The discovery will help predict space weather. What other secrets might these giant eruptions be hiding?
threads: Астрономы впервые поймали «радиоэхо» от магнитных узелков внутри исполинского облака солнечной плазмы. Это позволило измерить их невидимую силу — от 5.6 до 7.9 гаусс. Словно услышали, как звенит магнитная струна в космосе.

title: Star Soup from an Ancient Cluster
oneliner: Poorly mixed gas in the early Universe gave birth to stars that were anything but uniform.
description: In cluster M15, the first generation of stars shows a spread in heavy elements, while the second does not. This means the gas for the first stars was poorly mixed. This helps us understand where elements like gold are born: most likely in neutron star mergers, which happen quickly.
abstract: In the star cluster M15, astronomers measured the amounts of heavy elements. It turned out that the first stars, like a poorly stirred soup, had different amounts of these elements, while later ones were uniform. This hints at a turbulent and fast past for the cluster. Could this help us understand where gold in the Universe comes from?

title: Dark Energy: Constancy Questioned
oneliner: New data hints that dark energy may have changed over time.
description: Recent observations of supernovae, galaxy distribution, and the Big Bang afterglow show that dark energy density is not constant. This challenges the standard cosmological model and promises a revision of the Universe's evolution.
abstract: Scientists have found hints that dark energy — the mysterious force accelerating the expansion of the universe — is not constant, but changed over time. It's like a car that sometimes speeds up for no apparent reason. Analysis of telescope data and the cosmic microwave background shows that the familiar model may be cracking. So what is driving the cosmos?
threads: Вселенная расширяется с ускорением, но новое исследование намекает: это ускорение не постоянное — тёмная энергия может менять силу со временем, как порывы ветра. В далёком будущем это способно привести к неожиданному финалу космоса.

title: The Surprising Tail of Dying Light
oneliner: Quantum mechanics shows that a glowing dye's afterglow changes duration with the color you observe.
description: Like a stadium emptying, a fluorescent dye dims quickly, then slows to a trickle. The lingering glow isn't uniform: its pace depends on which color you watch. This quantum oddity reveals hidden exit routes for escaping light.
abstract: Picture a dying campfire: once the flames quit, you're left with sullen embers that fade slowly and unevenly. Researchers spotted a similar stunt in two glowing dyes. Long after the main light show ended, their gear picked up faint, rare flickers—a 'tail' of light that petered out differently depending on the color. Makes you wonder: could time in the quantum world tick at different speeds for different watchers?
threads: Fluorescent dyes fade with a long, unexpected glow that changes color. This 'quantum tail' depends on which shade you watch. A stadium emptying: most rush out, but a few linger, and the lingerers pick different exits.

title: Ultrahot planet hides under a gas blanket
oneliner: The Webb telescope discovered: planet TOI-561 b is wrapped in a dense gas blanket, even as its star scorches relentlessly.
description: Planet TOI-561 b orbits its star in less than a day and surprises with its lightness. Infrared observations revealed: the dayside isn't hot enough for bare rock. This means a thick gas coat holds in the heat, rewriting the rules for worlds around bright stars.
abstract: We used to think that planets zipping around their stars in just hours would be scorched down to bare rock. But TOI-561 b is a world with a dense gas shell that cools its sunlit side like a wet blanket on a red-hot surface. So even the fiercest stellar heat doesn't always destroy an atmosphere — and these worlds may still hide plenty of secrets inside.
threads: Планета TOI-561 b обегает звезду за 10 часов, но не стала голой скалой: плотная газовая оболочка окутывает её, как одеяло. Это переворот в планетологии: даже самые обожжённые миры могут сохранять атмосферу, пряча газы в океанах магмы.

title: Hunting for Light from Black Hole Crumbs
oneliner: Scientists tried to spot instantaneous flashes from tiny black holes born in giant collisions, but without success so far.
description: Black hole mergers can create tiny mini black holes that quickly evaporate, flashing with gamma-ray light. A search for such signals in the Fermi telescope archive came up empty, but it allowed to rule out certain masses for these crumbs.
abstract: Scientists looked for Hawking radiation from minuscule black holes that might be born when big ones crash together. Using data from the Fermi telescope, they watched the spot where black holes merged. They found nothing, but they were able to set limits on how tiny these holes can be. Picture an asteroid melting like an ice cube and giving off invisible light—so far, this light stays out of sight. Will we ever catch a glimpse of this cosmic vapor?
threads: Когда две массивные чёрные дыры сливаются, они могут разбрызгивать крошечные «крошки» — чёрные дыры астероидной массы. Такие малютки быстро испаряются, выдавая себя гамма-вспышками. Учёные проверили данные с телескопа Fermi после слияния GW170814 — и не нашли ни одной вспышки. Это значит, что крошки либо гораздо более редки, либо испаряются не так ярко, как предполагалось. Но уже готовятся более зоркие инструменты.

title: Quantum Duet: How Two Pendulums Catch a Shared Rhythm
oneliner: Two quantum 'pendulums' have synchronized for the first time, but their shared rhythm is only visible when measured together.
description: Classical synchronization is familiar from pendulum clocks that align their ticks via a common support. Scientists have now brought this effect into the quantum realm for the first time: two ions, cooled nearly to absolute zero, swung in unison thanks to finely tuned energy losses. The catch? You ca
abstract: Imagine pendulums that automatically adjust to each other—that's synchronization. Scientists have witnessed this kind of sync at the quantum level for the first time, between two special "atomic clocks." It turns out their harmony can't be glimpsed separately, only together—like a dance whose meaning is lost if you watch just one dancer. Could networks of such quantum oscillators spawn entirely new states?
threads: Два квантовых маятника впервые засинхронизировались, но их танец виден только при общем взгляде — словно музыканты, играющие дуэтом, который невозможно услышать поодиночке. Это открытие прокладывает путь к новым квантовым датчикам.

title: Gravitational Ringing Unveils Neutron Star Mysteries
oneliner: The fading gravitational echo from merged neutron stars can reveal the elasticity of matter in their interiors.
description: After neutron stars merge, a ringing clump of ultra-dense matter remains. Analyzing how fast this gravitational chiming fades relative to the spin-down rate allows, for the first time, measurement of matter’s elasticity in the interior, where densities are 5–6 times nuclear. Paradoxically, it can be
abstract: Scientists simulated neutron star mergers and found that the 'tail' of gravitational waves after the merger can reveal the properties of matter in the core. It's like telling what metal a bell is made of just from its fading ring. This link opens a path to understanding matter at extreme densities that we can't otherwise probe.
threads: После столкновения двух нейтронных звезд остается вибрирующий сгусток сверхплотной материи. Его гравитационный звон — угасающая рябь пространства — выдает секрет: по тому, как быстро тает вращение, можно вычислить, насколько жестка материя в ядре, где плотность в пять раз выше ядерной. Этот метод впервые откроет окно в мир самого экстремального вещества.

title: How Dark Energy Makes Black Holes Less Destructive
oneliner: Dark energy turns falling into a black hole from a catastrophe into a risky journey.
description: Calculations show: dark energy weakens tidal forces at the black hole’s horizon. So a large object has a chance to survive if the universe’s expansion is accelerating. Without dark energy, any body would be torn apart.
abstract: In an expanding universe, a black hole isn't so scary: a falling particle reaches its horizon in finite time. But without expansion, infinite tidal forces build up inside the horizon, and an extended body gets torn apart. Could anything survive such a plunge?
threads: Обычно чёрные дыры разрывают всё, что приближается к горизонту. Но новая работа показала: если во Вселенной есть тёмная энергия, падающий объект может остаться целым. Выходит, загадочное расширение мироздания способно защитить от губительных приливных сил.

title: Magnetar Confirms: The Vacuum Isn't Empty
oneliner: The ultra-strong magnetic field of a neutron star transforms empty space into a crystal-like state—and we've finally seen it.
description: The polarization of X-rays from a dead star with a monstrous magnetic field has been measured. It changes with energy as if the vacuum behaves like a crystal, bending light. This is the first direct evidence of a quantum effect predicted over 80 years ago.
abstract: Quantum electrodynamics predicts that empty vacuum in an ultra-strong magnetic field can change light's polarization. Scientists studied a magnetar (a neutron star with a record-breaking magnetic field) and saw unusually high polarization of its X-rays, which can only be explained by the vacuum's influence. So, does space around such stars act like a giant prism?
threads: Сверхсильное магнитное поле мёртвой звезды превращает пустоту в кристалл, который искривляет свет. Астрономы впервые заметили этот квантовый эффект, предсказанный 80 лет назад. Возможно, он поможет понять природу таинственных радиовсплесков из далёких галактик.

title: How to Make a Black Hole Perfectly Black
oneliner: By shaping incoming space ripples, scientists forced a black hole to absorb all energy, rendering it truly black.
description: Black holes usually reflect some waves. By shaping these waves to hit a precise resonance, physicists forced a black hole to absorb all energy, turning perfectly black. The absorbed energy is then released as a distinct ring, like a bell's tone revealing its shape. This technique could unveil the se
abstract: Physicists have discovered a way to make stars and black holes truly black, forcing them to absorb all incoming light without a trace. It’s like fine-tuning a sound so it completely fades away. This 'virtual absorption' stores energy like a spring, and then releases it—could this change how we think about black holes?
threads: Usually, black holes aren't completely black—they reflect some ripples in space. By fine-tuning these ripples like a singer shatters glass, physicists made a black hole swallow everything perfectly. It then releases the energy as a unique 'hum'. This could unlock secrets of spacetime.

title: Quantum Network Grabs Gravity by the Tail
oneliner: A web of entangled atoms acts like a hypersensitive spiderweb, catching the tiniest gravity shifts.
description: The study describes a quantum web of many atomic groups. Atoms within are entangled like knots in a spiderweb, reacting sensitively to gravity changes. The network can notice how gravity alters time’s flow: at different heights, clocks tick differently. This is a first step toward testing gravity’s
abstract: Scientists have proposed a quantum network, like a web of ultra-precise atomic clocks, to catch the tiniest gravitational changes. It could let us check how time flows differently at different heights and open new doors in understanding the quantum world and gravity. What do you think—could such a web catch a gravitational wave?
threads: Крошечная разница в высоте заставляет время течь чуть быстрее. Новый квантовый детектор из запутанных атомов улавливает это изменение, превращая сеть атомных часов в весы для гравитации. Это шаг к проверке, как соединяются квантовый мир и теория относительности.

title: How to See the Invisible: Recovering Polarization from a Handful of Photons
oneliner: A new method recovers the orientation of light waves from just a few particles of light, using machine learning and the patterns of light behavior.
description: The algorithm, like an artist, reconstructs the polarization picture from sparse strokes. Accounting for the smooth change of light properties from color to color allows it to work in extremely low light—this will improve astronomical observations and material analysis.
abstract: Scientists found a way to measure light polarization in ultra-low light conditions—when photons are so few that conventional methods fail. The new approach, akin to reconstructing a painting from a few brushstrokes, uses the relationships between adjacent data points and machine learning. This allows extracting detailed polarization information across wavelengths even in near-total darkness. Imagine: how can just a handful of photons reveal a material's hidden properties?
threads: Свет может быть таким тусклым, что обычные камеры почти слепнут. Но новый квантово-вдохновлённый метод учится видеть поляризацию всего по нескольким фотонам — как художник, угадывающий картину по редким мазкам. Это откроет путь к сверхчувствительным датчикам и астрономическим наблюдениям.

title: How Volunteers Help Unravel the Mysteries of Radio Galaxies
oneliner: Humans and AI team up to build the sharpest radio map of the heavens.
description: Volunteers and algorithms unite to sort millions of radio galaxies and build the most detailed map of the southern sky.
abstract: Radio telescopes see millions of objects, but telling which one is an active galaxy and which is just noise is very hard—like distinguishing a cat from a dog in a blurry photo. The Radio Galaxy Zoo EMU project has enlisted volunteers to help scientists, and smart algorithms double-check their answers. Will we manage to map all the hidden galaxies of the southern sky?
threads: Огромный радиотелескоп ASKAP каждую ночь ловит шёпот миллионов галактик. Разобрать этот хор по голосам учёным помогают тысячи добровольцев. Их отметки учат нейросети, и скоро появится самая подробная радиокарта южного неба.

title: A Giant Black Hole's Wake in a Spiral Galaxy
oneliner: Astronomers have spotted a huge gas trail in galaxy NGC 3627, resembling the wake of a speedboat.
description: A very long and narrow trail of gas and dust has been discovered in the galaxy NGC 3627. It stretches for 26,000 light-years but is only 650 light-years wide. This wake was left by a massive black hole or the core of a dwarf galaxy that plunged through the galactic disk. The discovery offers a new m
abstract: Astronomers have spotted a giant gas trail in galaxy NGC 3627 that looks like a contrail from an airplane. It's 40 times longer than it is wide and stretches for thousands of light-years. This long, narrow 'condensation' trail was left behind by a massive object that tore through the galaxy's disk—maybe a black hole or the core of a dwarf galaxy. Now, trails like this could become a way to find hidden giants in space.
threads: Астрономы нашли гигантский след в галактике, похожий на шлейф от самолёта. Он тянется на 80 тысяч световых лет! Такой след могла оставить чёрная дыра или маленькая галактика, пролетевшая сквозь газовое облако.

title: Two Times in the Quantum World
oneliner: Scientists compare two approaches to the nature of time: timeless and with two directions — perhaps our reality is just their meeting point.
description: In quantum mechanics, time ceases to be a familiar arrow. One approach claims all moments already exist simultaneously. Another method suggests particles can move backward in time. Comparing these ideas opens the door to understanding the true fabric of reality.
threads: Обычно время течёт как река, но в квантовом мире физики спорят: можно ли повернуть его вспять? Один подход говорит, что мир застыл, всё уже случилось. Другой допускает, что частицы живут сразу вперёд и назад во времени. Проверка этой идеи однажды может дать ключ к управлению временем.

title: Black Holes Remember Everything: Memory Burden Alters Their Oscillations
oneliner: Information inside a black hole isn’t dead weight — it’s what makes it tremble in a peculiar way.
description: Scientists have theoretically demonstrated that the information accumulated by a black hole (its “memory burden”) affects its oscillations under external perturbations. Two black holes of identical mass but with different information loads emit different gravitational wave spectra. This discovery pr
abstract: Imagine a black hole as a cosmic hard drive. It turns out, the more information it stores, the differently it vibrates when shaken. This property, dubbed the 'memory burden effect,' can be observed during black hole collisions and even replicated in the lab with cold atoms.
